start.title: The Impact Quest
start.narrative:
You're Dr. Alex Chen, a researcher at a UK university. Your team has just published a groundbreaking study on how community-led urban green spaces reduce anxiety and improve social cohesion in deprived neighbourhoods.

The paper is strong — published in a top journal with robust methodology. But right now, it's just words on a page. Your Head of School has flagged your work as having “REF impact potential” and the university's Impact Officer has reached out.

The clock is ticking toward REF 2029. What you do next will determine whether this research changes lives — or gathers dust.

choices.text: Meet with the Impact Officer to develop a strategy
Go straight to Twitter/X and start promoting the paper
Contact the local council directly — they need to hear this

meet_officer.title: Strategic Thinking
meet_officer.narrative:
Smart move. The Impact Officer, Sam, is enthusiastic. Over coffee, they help you map out a Theory of Change — connecting your research findings to potential real-world outcomes.

Sam explains: “We need to think about WHO could use this research, HOW they'd use it, and what EVIDENCE we'd need to prove it made a difference.”

Together you identify three potential impact pathways:
• Policy influence — informing local or national green space policy
• Practitioner change — helping community organisations design better programmes
• Public benefit — directly improving wellbeing in target communities

Sam asks: “Which pathway do you want to prioritise first?”

choices.text: Policy influence — let's aim high and target policymakers
Practitioner change — let's work with community organisations on the ground
Can we pursue all three at once?

social_media_first.title: Going Viral (Sort Of)
social_media_first.narrative:
You write a Twitter thread about your research. It gets 200 likes, a few retweets from other academics, and one reply from someone saying “this is obvious lol.”

A journalist from a regional paper picks it up and writes a short piece: “University boffins discover parks are good for you.” It's not exactly the nuanced coverage you hoped for.

The reach is broad but shallow. You've raised awareness, but awareness alone isn't impact — at least not the kind REF panels are looking for. You need to show your research actually CHANGED something.

Your Impact Officer, Sam, sends you a gentle email: “Great to see the media coverage! Shall we meet to discuss turning this attention into something more strategic?”

choices.text: Take Sam up on the offer — time to get strategic
Double down on media — pitch to national outlets
A community group has commented on the article — reach out to them

cold_call_council.title: The Cold Shoulder
cold_call_council.narrative:
You email the local council's environment team with your paper attached and a note saying “This could transform your green space strategy!”

Three weeks pass. Nothing.

You follow up. A junior officer replies: “Thanks for sending this. We'll keep it on file.” That's council-speak for the recycling bin.

The problem? You approached them without understanding their priorities, timelines, or how they actually make decisions. Policy engagement is a different skill from academic publishing.

Your colleague mentions that the university has a Policy Engagement training session next month, run by the Impact and Engagement team.

choices.text: Attend the policy engagement training
Try a different approach — find a personal connection at the council
Forget the council — focus on community organisations instead

meet_officer_late.title: Better Late Than Never
meet_officer_late.narrative:
Sam doesn't judge. “The media coverage actually gives us something to work with,” they say. “But we need to move from awareness to action.”

Sam helps you develop a stakeholder map and a proper engagement plan. You identify that your research is most relevant to:

• The council's Public Health team (not Environment — they don't have budget)
• Three local community organisations already running green space projects
• A national charity that funds urban greening initiatives

“The key,” Sam says, “is to listen first. Find out what THEY need, then show how your research helps.”

choices.text: Start with the community organisations — build from the ground up
Target the national charity — they could fund a bigger project

media_blitz.title: Diminishing Returns
media_blitz.narrative:
You pitch to The Guardian, BBC, and several national outlets. One radio station invites you for a 3-minute interview at 6:40am. You do it half-asleep and stumble over the key findings.

A few more articles appear, but they all say roughly the same thing. Your university's comms team is pleased, but when you talk to Sam the Impact Officer, they're candid:

“Media coverage is great for public engagement, but REF panels want to see your research actually changed behaviour, practice, or policy. A newspaper article isn't evidence of impact — it's evidence of dissemination.”

This lands hard. You've been busy, but have you been effective?

choices.text: Regroup with Sam and build a proper impact strategy
A council officer saw the BBC piece and got in touch — follow up

community_contact.title: A Grassroots Connection
community_contact.narrative:
The community group — GreenSpace Collective — runs urban gardens in three deprived wards. Their coordinator, Priya, is excited about your research.

“We've been telling the council for years that our gardens help people's mental health, but we never had the evidence. Your study could change everything for us.”

Priya invites you to visit their sites and meet participants. You spend a Saturday morning pulling weeds and chatting to people whose lives have been genuinely transformed by the green spaces.

This is powerful. But how do you turn a Saturday morning into REF-quality impact?

choices.text: Co-design a wellbeing measurement tool with GreenSpace Collective
Help them write a funding bid using your research evidence
Invite Priya to give a guest lecture at the university

policy_route.title: The Policy Pathway
policy_route.narrative:
Sam connects you with the university's Policy Engagement lead, who helps you craft a policy brief — a concise, jargon-free summary of your findings with clear recommendations.

You learn that effective policy engagement means:
• Understanding the policy cycle and timing your interventions
• Speaking their language, not yours
• Offering solutions, not just findings
• Building relationships over time, not just sending documents

The policy brief is excellent. Now you need to get it in front of the right people. The local council is refreshing its Health & Wellbeing Strategy, and there's a public consultation opening next month.

choices.text: Submit a formal response to the consultation using your evidence
Request a meeting with the Director of Public Health
Both — submit the response AND request the meeting

practitioner_route.title: Working With Communities
practitioner_route.narrative:
You connect with three community organisations running green space projects across the region. Each has different needs:

• GreenSpace Collective wants evidence to support their funding bids
• Park Life CIC wants to redesign their programme based on what actually works
• The Neighbourhood Trust wants to train volunteers using evidence-based approaches

All three are enthusiastic, but you're one person with a full teaching load and two PhD students to supervise. Sam warns: “Don't overcommit. One deep partnership beats three shallow ones for REF purposes.”

choices.text: Go deep with GreenSpace Collective — co-produce new resources
Work with Park Life CIC to redesign their programme
Try to support all three — you can manage it

all_three.title: Ambition vs. Reality
all_three.narrative:
Sam pauses. “I love the ambition, but let me be honest — trying to do everything at once usually means nothing gets done well. REF panels can spot a mile-wide, inch-deep impact narrative immediately.”

“The strongest impact case studies tell a focused story: clear pathway, strong evidence, demonstrable change. We can absolutely pursue multiple pathways eventually, but let's nail one first.”

This is good advice. Which pathway has the most potential right now?

choices.text: Policy influence — there's a council consultation coming up
Practitioner change — community groups are already interested

policy_training.title: Learning the Craft
policy_training.narrative:
The policy engagement training is a revelation. You learn about:

• How policy is actually made (spoiler: it's messier than you thought)
• The difference between policy influence and policy change
• How to write a briefing note that a civil servant will actually read
• The importance of timing — policy windows open and close

Crucially, you learn that the council is about to refresh its Health & Wellbeing Strategy. There's a 12-week public consultation starting next month. This is your window.

You also meet a colleague from Public Health who has contacts in the council's wellbeing team.

choices.text: Prepare a formal consultation response with your evidence
Ask your Public Health colleague for an introduction to the council team

find_connection.title: The Warm Introduction
find_connection.narrative:
You mention your council frustrations at a faculty event, and a colleague in Public Health says: “Oh, you need to talk to Janet in the council's wellbeing team. We collaborated on a project last year. Let me introduce you.”

This is how policy engagement actually works — through relationships and trust, not cold emails.

Janet agrees to a coffee meeting. She's frank: “We get dozens of research papers sent to us. What makes yours different is that you're here, asking what we need, rather than telling us what you've found.”

Janet reveals that the council is developing a new Green Space Action Plan.

choices.text: Offer to present your findings to Janet's team
Ask Janet what evidence gaps the council has — tailor your input to their needs

warm_intro.title: The Warm Introduction
warm_intro.narrative:
Your colleague introduces you to Janet, a senior officer in the council's wellbeing team. Janet agrees to meet for coffee.

She's refreshingly direct: “Academics usually tell us what they've found and expect us to be grateful. What would actually help is if you could tell us what WORKS and for WHOM — in plain English, with costs.”

She mentions the council is developing a new Green Space Action Plan and they're short on evidence about mental health outcomes.

choices.text: Offer to present your findings to Janet's team
Ask what specific evidence gaps the council needs filled

consultation.title: The Formal Response
consultation.narrative:
You submit a detailed, evidence-based response to the Health & Wellbeing Strategy consultation. It's well-crafted, citing your research and making clear, actionable recommendations about green space investment.

Two months later, the final strategy is published. Buried in the evidence annex, your paper is cited. One of your recommendations about “community-led green space programmes” appears in the action plan, though reworded.

This is genuine policy influence! But the evidence trail is thin — how do you prove your research specifically contributed to this change?

choices.text: Request a letter from the council acknowledging your research contribution
Write a follow-up FOI request to see how consultation responses were used
Meet with Janet at the council to discuss next steps and document the connection

consultation_and_meeting.title: Belt and Braces
consultation_and_meeting.narrative:
Excellent instinct. You submit the consultation response AND secure a meeting with the Director of Public Health (DPH).

The DPH is interested: “We know green spaces matter, but we've struggled to make the economic case. Can your research help us model the cost-benefit?”

This is a golden opportunity — but it's also scope creep. Cost-benefit analysis wasn't part of your original study. However, your colleague in Health Economics might be able to help.

Meanwhile, your consultation response is logged formally, creating a paper trail.

choices.text: Collaborate with Health Economics to produce the cost-benefit analysis
Stick to what your research actually shows — don't overreach

dph_meeting.title: Aiming High
dph_meeting.narrative:
Getting a meeting with the Director of Public Health takes some persistence, but your Impact Officer Sam uses a university contact to broker an introduction.

The DPH gives you 30 minutes. You present your findings clearly. They're engaged but pragmatic: “This is interesting, but I need to know: what would you recommend we actually DO differently based on this?”

This is the moment. Policy makers don't want data dumps — they want actionable recommendations.

choices.text: Recommend the council funds community-led green space programmes in deprived wards
Suggest a pilot project to test your approach in one neighbourhood first

codesign.title: Co-Production Gold
codesign.narrative:
You work with GreenSpace Collective over six months to co-design a wellbeing measurement toolkit. It's based on your research but shaped by the community's own language and priorities.

The toolkit is practical, accessible, and genuinely useful. GreenSpace Collective starts using it across their three sites. Other organisations hear about it and want copies.

Priya says: “For the first time, we can actually show funders what difference we make. This has transformed how we work.”

This is strong impact evidence — practice change driven by your research. But you need to document it properly.

choices.text: Commission independent testimonials and a case study from GreenSpace Collective
Track the toolkit's adoption — how many organisations are using it?
Apply for follow-on funding to scale the toolkit nationally

programme_redesign.title: Evidence Into Practice
programme_redesign.narrative:
Park Life CIC works with you to redesign their community programme using your evidence. They restructure their sessions to include the elements your research identified as most beneficial: social connection time, nature contact, and participant-led activities.

After six months, their participant satisfaction scores increase significantly, and they report better mental health outcomes among attendees.

The CIC's director writes to you: “Your research has fundamentally changed how we deliver our programme. We're seeing real differences in people's lives.”

choices.text: Document this with before/after data and testimonials
Help Park Life present this at a national community health conference

overcommit.title: Stretched Too Thin
overcommit.narrative:
You try to support all three organisations simultaneously. For two months, you're racing between meetings, missing deadlines, and sending rushed emails.

GreenSpace Collective's Priya sends a carefully worded message: “We really value the partnership, but we haven't seen the draft toolkit you promised in January. We need it for our March funding bid.”

Park Life stops replying to your emails. The Neighbourhood Trust moves on and finds a consultant instead.

Sam checks in: “How's it going?” You're honest: it's not going well.

“It's not too late to refocus,” Sam says. “Pick one and do it properly.”

choices.text: Recommit to GreenSpace Collective — apologise and deliver
Reconnect with Park Life CIC — they had the clearest need

council_inbound.title: An Unexpected Ally
council_inbound.narrative:
A council officer named Janet saw your BBC interview and reaches out: “We're developing a new Green Space Action Plan and your research is exactly what we need. Could we meet?”

This is the dream scenario — a policy maker coming to YOU. But you need to handle it well. Janet's interest is genuine but time-limited; if you don't deliver value quickly, the moment will pass.

choices.text: Meet Janet immediately and ask what evidence she needs
Prepare a polished briefing document first, then meet

council_presentation.title: Making Your Case
council_presentation.narrative:
You present your findings to the council's wellbeing team. You've learned from earlier mistakes — no jargon, clear recommendations, costs and benefits laid out simply.

The team is engaged. Two things happen:

1. Your evidence is incorporated into the council's new Green Space Action Plan
2. Janet asks if you'd sit on an advisory panel for the plan's implementation

This is significant policy influence. Your research is directly shaping council strategy. But for REF purposes, you'll need written evidence of this influence.

choices.text: Accept the advisory role and request formal documentation of your contribution
Focus on getting a letter from the council confirming the research influence

needs_led.title: Listening First
needs_led.narrative:
You spend an hour listening to Janet describe the council's challenges. It turns out they need evidence on three things:

1. Which types of green space interventions are most cost-effective
2. How to target provision at communities with the greatest need
3. Whether community-led approaches work better than council-managed ones

Your research directly addresses points 2 and 3. Point 1 would need additional analysis, but your Health Economics colleague could help.

Janet is impressed that you asked rather than assumed. “This is how academics should work with us,” she says.

choices.text: Produce a tailored evidence briefing addressing all three questions
Focus only on what your research covers (points 2 and 3) — stay credible

charity_route.title: Following the Money
charity_route.narrative:
You approach GreenFutures, a national charity that funds urban greening. Their research lead is interested in your work and invites you to present at their annual conference.

The conference goes well. GreenFutures asks if you'd co-develop an evidence framework for assessing community green space projects — essentially embedding your research into how they evaluate and fund programmes nationally.

This could mean your research influences dozens of projects across the country.

choices.text: Accept — this is national-scale impact
Accept, but also maintain the local community partnerships

funding_bid.title: Empowering Partners
funding_bid.narrative:
You help GreenSpace Collective write a funding bid to the National Lottery, using your research as the evidence base. The bid makes a compelling case: here's the research showing this approach works, and here's the community organisation ready to deliver it.

The bid is successful — £150,000 over three years. Priya is ecstatic. Your research has directly enabled this community organisation to expand their life-changing work.

This is tangible, evidenced impact. The funding decision letter explicitly cites your research as key supporting evidence.

choices.text: Document this carefully — the funding letter is gold-standard evidence
Negotiate a research partnership within the funded project

guest_lecture.title: Nice, But...
guest_lecture.narrative:
Priya gives a great guest lecture. Students love hearing from a practitioner. But Sam gently points out:

“A guest lecture is a lovely thing, but it's not really impact — it's just internal university activity. Impact means your research changed something OUT THERE in the world.

“Priya's visit is actually more valuable as a relationship-building step. What can you do WITH her organisation, not just have them talk TO your students?”

choices.text: You're right — let's co-design something with GreenSpace Collective
Actually, could this lead to a Knowledge Transfer Partnership?

cost_benefit.title: Strengthening the Case
cost_benefit.narrative:
Your Health Economics colleague helps produce a cost-benefit analysis showing that every £1 invested in community-led green spaces generates £3.20 in health and social benefits.

The DPH's eyes light up: “THIS is what I can take to cabinet. They don't understand wellbeing scores, but they understand return on investment.”

The council commits £500,000 to a new Community Green Space Programme, explicitly citing your research and the cost-benefit analysis in the cabinet paper.

This is major policy impact with clear evidence.

choices.text: Secure copies of the cabinet paper and decision documents
Offer to evaluate the new programme — creating a research cycle

stay_focused.title: Credibility Preserved
stay_focused.narrative:
You resist the temptation to overreach and focus on what your research actually demonstrates. Your briefing is authoritative, honest about limitations, and includes clear recommendations.

Janet appreciates the integrity: “So many people tell us what they think we want to hear. You've told us what the evidence actually says, including the gaps.”

Your evidence is incorporated into the council's strategy with a clear acknowledgment. Janet recommends you to colleagues in two neighbouring councils facing similar challenges.

choices.text: Engage with the neighbouring councils to extend your reach
Apply for funding to address the evidence gaps Janet identified

bold_recommendation.title: Fortune Favours the Bold
bold_recommendation.narrative:
The DPH pushes back initially: “Community-led? We can't just hand over money without accountability.”

But you're prepared. You present your evidence showing community-led approaches have better outcomes AND better cost-effectiveness than top-down provision. You share the GreenSpace Collective's data.

Three months later, the council announces a £300,000 Community Green Space Fund — ring-fenced for community organisations in deprived wards. Your research is cited as the evidence base.

Local headlines read: “Council backs community green spaces after university research.”

choices.text: Document everything — cabinet papers, press coverage, the funding decision

pilot_project.title: Testing the Waters
pilot_project.narrative:
The DPH likes the pilot idea. It's lower risk and produces fresh evidence. The council agrees to fund a 12-month pilot in one ward, with your team providing the evaluation framework.

The pilot is a success. Participants show significant improvements in wellbeing, and the community group running it demonstrates it can be delivered cost-effectively.

The DPH uses the pilot results to secure full council funding for a borough-wide rollout. Your pilot has become a programme.

choices.text: This is perfect — document the pilot-to-programme journey as your impact narrative

tailored_briefing.title: Delivering Value
tailored_briefing.narrative:
You produce a superb evidence briefing that directly addresses Janet's three questions. For the cost-effectiveness question, your Health Economics colleague contributes a preliminary analysis.

The briefing lands perfectly. Janet circulates it to the senior leadership team. Three weeks later, you're invited to present to the council's Health & Wellbeing Board.

Your research is directly shaping how the council allocates its green space budget. This is exactly the kind of impact REF panels look for.

choices.text: Present to the Board and secure documentary evidence of influence

advisory_panel.title: Embedded Influence
advisory_panel.narrative:
You join the advisory panel. Over 18 months, your research shapes multiple council decisions about green space investment and design. You keep meticulous records of meetings, recommendations made, and actions taken.

The council's Green Space Action Plan explicitly acknowledges your advisory role and cites your research 14 times. The plan redirects £2 million in capital spending toward community-led projects.

Sam is delighted: “This is textbook policy impact. You've got the evidence trail, the policy documents, the scale — this could be a flagship case study.”

choices.text: Compile the impact case study with Sam's help

evidence_letter.title: Building the Evidence Base
evidence_letter.narrative:
You request a formal letter from the council's Director of Public Health confirming how your research influenced their strategy. The letter is specific and powerful, citing exact recommendations that were adopted.

Sam helps you file this alongside the consultation documents, the strategy itself (with your research cited), and the before/after comparison showing what changed.

“This is solid evidential gold,” Sam says. “A REF panel wants to see exactly this kind of documentary evidence — third-party confirmation of your research's influence.”

choices.text: Continue building the case — what other evidence should we gather?

strong_evidence.title: The Evidence Portfolio
strong_evidence.narrative:
You systematically build an evidence portfolio:

• Testimonial letters from partner organisations describing specific changes
• Before/after data showing improved outcomes
• The wellbeing toolkit (or funding letter, or programme evaluation) as tangible outputs
• Records of adoption by other organisations
• Independent evaluation data

Sam reviews it all: “This is excellent. You've got corroborated evidence from multiple sources, showing a clear causal chain from your research to real-world change. This is exactly what makes a strong impact case study.”

choices.text: Write up the impact case study

track_adoption.title: Measuring Spread
track_adoption.narrative:
You create a simple tracking system for toolkit adoption. Within a year:

• 12 community organisations across the region are using your toolkit
• Two national charities have incorporated it into their evaluation frameworks
• A local authority in another city has adopted it as standard practice

The reach is impressive and growing. Each adopter provides another piece of evidence for your impact case study. Sam helps you map this adoption, showing the “ripple effect” from your original research.

choices.text: Document the full adoption story for the impact case study

scale_up.title: Going National
scale_up.narrative:
You secure UKRI follow-on funding to scale the toolkit nationally, partnering with a network of community organisations. The project reaches 50+ organisations across England.

A national evaluation shows the toolkit is improving how community groups measure and communicate their impact, leading to more successful funding bids and better-targeted services.

Your original research has catalysed a national movement in evidence-based community green space practice.

choices.text: This is a landmark impact story — write it up

national_framework.title: Systemic Change
national_framework.narrative:
You co-develop GreenFutures' new evaluation framework. It's adopted as standard practice across their £10 million annual grants portfolio, meaning every project they fund is now assessed using criteria derived from your research.

This is systemic impact — your research has changed how an entire sector operates. The framework is subsequently picked up by two other national funders.

Sam is thrilled: “National reach, multiple beneficiaries, clear evidence of change in practice. This is REF gold.”

choices.text: Compile the impact case study

dual_track.title: Balancing Act
dual_track.narrative:
You manage to maintain both the national charity work and local community partnerships — but only because you get a part-time research assistant funded through the university's Impact Acceleration Account.

The two tracks complement each other: local partners provide authentic stories and ground-level evidence, while the national work provides reach and systemic change.

After two years, you have an impact story that works at both local and national scale.

choices.text: Write the impact case study — you've got a compelling dual narrative

embedded_research.title: A Virtuous Cycle
embedded_research.narrative:
You negotiate a research partnership within the funded project. This means you can track outcomes in real-time, co-produce knowledge with the community, and generate new research outputs.

The funded project delivers excellent results. Your embedded evaluation provides new evidence that feeds into your next publication AND strengthens the impact evidence for REF.

You've created a virtuous cycle: research → impact → new research → greater impact.

choices.text: Document this as your impact case study — it's a powerful narrative

ktp_idea.title: Thinking Bigger
ktp_idea.narrative:
A Knowledge Transfer Partnership could place a dedicated associate at GreenSpace Collective, embedding your research into their practice over two years. Sam loves the idea and helps you scope it.

The KTP application is successful. Over 24 months, the associate transforms how GreenSpace Collective operates — implementing evidence-based programme design, robust outcomes measurement, and a sustainability strategy.

The Collective grows from 3 sites to 8, secures three major grants, and becomes a national exemplar of evidence-based community practice. All grounded in your research.

choices.text: This KTP story IS the impact case study — write it up

conference_impact.title: Amplifying the Story
conference_impact.narrative:
Park Life presents at a national community health conference to 200 delegates. Their presentation — co-authored with you — shows the before/after transformation with compelling data and participant stories.

Three other organisations approach Park Life to learn how they redesigned their programme. A public health commissioner in the audience asks for your contact details.

The ripple effect is growing. Your research is travelling through practitioner networks in ways you couldn't achieve through academic channels alone.

choices.text: Document this spreading influence for the impact case study

regional_impact.title: Spreading Influence
regional_impact.narrative:
You engage with the two neighbouring councils. Both adopt elements of your evidence into their own green space strategies. One commissions you to conduct a local evaluation.

Your impact has spread across three local authority areas, reaching a combined population of over 500,000. Each council provides documentary evidence of how your research influenced their decisions.

The regional story is compelling: one piece of research, multiple policy changes, growing community benefit.

choices.text: Write the impact case study — you've got a strong regional narrative

new_research.title: The Research Cycle Continues
new_research.narrative:
You secure funding to address the evidence gaps Janet identified. This new research, grounded in real policy needs, produces findings that are immediately useful to practitioners and policymakers.

Your reputation as a researcher who produces actionable, policy-relevant evidence grows. More councils and organisations seek your input.

Sam observes: “You've built something rare — a genuine, ongoing relationship between research and practice. The REF impact case study almost writes itself.”

choices.text: Write up the full impact story

foi_route.title: The Paper Trail
foi_route.narrative:
The FOI response reveals internal council documents showing your consultation response was flagged as “key evidence” and directly informed two of the strategy's priority actions.

This is strong documentary evidence — you can show, in the council's own words, that your research shaped their strategy.

Sam nods: “FOI responses are underused as impact evidence. This is the council confirming your influence in their own internal documents. A REF panel will love this.”

choices.text: Add this to the evidence portfolio and finalise the case study

ending_policy_star.title: Impact Case Study: Policy Influencer
ending_policy_star.narrative:
Congratulations! You've developed a strong policy-focused impact case study.

Your research journey shows a clear pathway from academic publication to real-world policy change. You built relationships with decision-makers, engaged at the right time, provided evidence in the right format, and documented everything.

Key lessons from your journey:
• Strategy matters — random outreach rarely works
• Listen to what stakeholders need, don't just broadcast your findings
• Timing is everything in policy engagement
• Evidence of impact needs to be gathered AS YOU GO, not retrofitted
• Your Impact Officer is your best friend in this process

ending_practitioner_star.title: Impact Case Study: Practice Transformer
ending_practitioner_star.narrative:
Congratulations! You've developed a compelling practice-based impact case study.

Your research has genuinely changed how organisations work, leading to better outcomes for communities. You co-produced knowledge with partners, embedded your research into real-world practice, and built evidence of transformative change.

Key lessons from your journey:
• Co-production with partners creates the strongest impact
• Deep partnerships beat shallow ones
• Let practitioners lead — your research supports their expertise
• Tangible outputs (toolkits, frameworks, redesigned programmes) are powerful evidence
• Impact takes time — invest in relationships

ending_full_circle.title: Impact Case Study: The Virtuous Cycle
ending_full_circle.narrative:
Congratulations! You've achieved something remarkable — a self-sustaining cycle where research drives impact, and impact drives new research.

Your impact case study shows research that didn't just change the world once — it created an ongoing relationship between evidence and practice that continues to generate benefits.

Key lessons from your journey:
• The best impact isn't a one-off event — it's an ongoing relationship
• Evaluation of your impact can generate new research questions
• Follow-on funding can amplify your original investment
• Building trust with partners creates opportunities you can't plan for
• Impact and research quality reinforce each other
